revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Keyhole-shaped kofun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:bump 1-20]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Keyhole-shaped kofun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Keyhole-shaped kofun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Keyhole-shaped kofun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:竪穴式石室]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:竪穴式石室]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後円墳]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:竪穴式石室]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:竪穴式石室]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Infobox Kofun of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Ryōzan Kofun}} |Image = |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}} {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} |Artifacts = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|fangzhuo|ja|方格規矩鏡}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka Kofun|ja|五色塚古墳}} (Hyogo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun Group]] [[Category:Kofun in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nara City]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Imperial mausolea in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:U]][[Category:c]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: tier 5]][[Category: c]][[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category: categories]] [[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:竪穴式石室]] [[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[sn:Saki Ryōzan Kofun]][[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]]

{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum shrine}} '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumulus|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, {{ill|Nara City|ja|奈良市}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内庁}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|ja|垂仁天皇}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} records the first instance of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|ja|宮内省}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部}}.
