Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south |location = {{ill|Misasagi-chō|WD=Q85879846}} |coordinates = {{Coord|34.69965555555556|N|135.78906388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Hibasuhimenomikoto|WD=Q8375821}} |part_of = {{ill|Saki-Tatenami Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11383489}} }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}}}} '''Saki Misasagiyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} records the first instance of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}}}} '''Saki Misasagiyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} records the first instance of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} records the first instance of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo|1=TLV mirror}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical shaft chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} |Deity = (Determined size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/Ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south |location = {{ill|Misasagi-chō|WD=Q85879846|1=Misasagi-chō}} |coordinates = {{Coord|34.69965555555556|N|135.78906388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Hibasuhimenomikoto|WD=Q8375821|1=Hibasu-hime}} |part_of = {{ill|Saki-Tatenami Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11383489|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}} }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}}}} '''Saki Misasagiyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} records the first instance of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo|1=TLV mirror}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo|1=TLV mirror}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun]][[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]][[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun]][[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]][[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun]][[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]][[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site of Japan |Name = {{ill|Saki Ryoyama Kofun|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|1=Saki Misasagiyama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ryoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ryōzan Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383490}} |image =File:Hibasuhimenomikoto sakinoteramanomisasagi2.jpg |Part of = {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}} |Location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}} {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}} Yamamori-cho |Latitude_deg = 34 |Latitude_min = 41 |Latitude_sec = 58.76 |Longitude_deg = 135 |Longitude_min = 47 |Longitude_sec = 20.63 |ISO = JP-29 |Shape = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} |Size = Total mound length: 207 m |Construction = Late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century|WD=Q8099|lt=4th century|1=4th century}} |Burial = {{ill|Vertical shaft chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical shaft chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} |Deity = (Determined by {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}) {{ill|Hibasuhime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}} |burial goods = Bronze mirrors, haniwa |Imperial_tomb = {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Notes = Ranked 32nd in Japan for size[https://www.city.sakai.lg.jp/kanko/rekishi/dkofun/Ranking/zenkoku.html Ancient Kofun Size Rankings (Japan)] (Sakai City official website, updated May 13, 2018). |Map = Nara City |Label = Saki Ryoyama
Kofun |Label_position = bottom |Icon = Keyhole-shaped tumulus-south |location = {{ill|Misasagi-chō|WD=Q85879846|1=Misasagi-chō}} |coordinates = {{Coord|34.69965555555556|N|135.78906388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architectural_styles = {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpōkōenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}} |commemorates = {{ill|Hibasuhimenomikoto|WD=Q8375821|1=Hibasu-hime}} |part_of = {{ill|Saki-Tatenami Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11383489|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}} }} [[File:790911 Ishiduka Misasagiyama Takatsuka.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}} (top left), '''Saki Ryoyama Kofun''' (top right), {{ill|Saki Takatsuka Kofun|ja|佐紀高塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Takatsuka Kofun|qq=}} (bottom left) (1979)
{{National Air Photograph}}]] [[File:Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, haisho.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}} Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum Shrine.}}]] {{panoViewer|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun, Haisho 360-degree.jpg|360-degree image of Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Haisho|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho|qq=}}}} '''Saki Misasagiyama Kofun''' (佐紀陵山古墳) is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} located in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara City|1=Nara (city)}}, Yamamori-cho. It is part of the {{ill|Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}. Although the actual individual interred is unknown, it has been designated by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} as the {{ill|Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja|狭木之寺間陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki-no-Terama Mausoleum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the resting place of {{ill|Hibasu-hime|ja|日葉酢媛命|1=Hibasu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibasu-hime|qq=}}, empress of the 11th emperor {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}. A significant looting event in the Taisho era prompted extensive investigations and restoration. == Overview == The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} records the first instance of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} being placed on this kofun instead of human sacrifice. Saki Ryoyama Kofun's rear mound encroaches into the constricted moat of the adjacent {{ill|Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|ja|佐紀石塚山古墳|1=Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Ishizukayama Kofun|qq=}}, suggesting the latter was constructed earlier. The mound measures 207 meters in total length, with a rear circle diameter of 131 meters and height of 20 meters, while the front section spans 87 meters in width and rises 12.3 meters.Kawakami, "Kofun of Yamato" (1984), pp. 42–43. In 1915, a large-scale looting incident resulted in the theft of artifacts. The perpetrators were later arrested, and the stolen artifacts were recovered. The following year, restoration works by the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}} included detailed surveys and records of the burial chamber and recovered items despite restrictions due to the tomb's status.Nakai, "Ancient Ruins of Japan" (1982), pp. 145–146. === Investigation During Restoration === Most records from the restoration, originally held by the Imperial Household Agency in Tokyo, were lost in the {{ill|Great Kantō Earthquake|ja|関東大震災|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Kantō Earthquake|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3742341}}. However, copies and artifact replicas made under archaeologists such as {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}} at Kyoto University were preserved and are now housed at the {{ill|Imperial Household Archives|ja|書陵部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Household Archives|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮内庁書陵部|1=Archives and Mausolea Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346533}}. Artifact casts, photos, and sketches remain, while looted items were returned to the tomb. === Chamber Structure === The burial chamber lies beneath the central mound and measures 8.55 meters in length and 1.09 meters in width. Constructed with flat stones and capped by five roof stones, it is one of the largest vertical shaft chambers in Japan. The southern and northern walls each feature a single massive stone slab with unique holes.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), pp. 117–124. === Artifacts and Dating === Artifacts recovered include: * Bronze mirrors: Three {{ill|TLV mirror|en|TLV mirror|WD=Q3316102|lt=fangzhuo|1=TLV mirror}}, including cloud-patterned and floral designs. * Stone ornaments: Rings, tools, and vessels. * Rare haniwa, including shield-shaped and cap-shaped examples.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 122. These suggest the kofun dates to the late early Kofun period.Mori, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (2011), p. 124. == Related Sites == Similar kofun include: * {{ill|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|en|Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|WD=Q1538541|lt=Goshikizuka Kofun|1=Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun}} (Hyōgo Prefecture) * {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun|ja|茶臼山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun|qq=}} (Otsu City, Shiga Prefecture) == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * {{ill|Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kawakami Kunihiko|lt=Kunihiko Kawakami|ja|河上邦彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunihiko Kawakami|qq=}}, "The Tomb of Hibasuhime," in ''Discussing Yamato's Kofun'' (Rokko Publishing, 1984), pp. 42–43. * {{ill|Sueji Umehara|ja|梅原末治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueji Umehara|qq=}}, "Ancient Imperial Tombs" (Chikuma Publishing, 2011), pp. 117–124. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} {{osm box|w|364093588}} * [http://www.kashikoken.jp/museum/yamatonoiseki/kofun/sakimisasagiyama.html] {{translated page|ja|佐紀陵山古墳|version=98265439|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{Commons category|Saki Misasagi-yama Kofun}} [[zh:佐紀陵山古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11383490}} [[Category:History of Nara]] [[Category:Japanese imperial tombs]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Saki Tatenami Kofun group]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]
